explanation: A Bash login shell reads /etc/profile first, then the first existing file among ~/.bash_profile, ~/.bash_login, and ~/.profile. ~/.bashrc is intended for non-login interactive shells and is not read directly during login unless one of those login files explicitly sources it. The other options are all standard login-shell startup files that Bash may read directly.

When a login shell reads ~/.profile, it executes it as a shell script, so the contents must use valid shell syntax. It does not need to be executable, start with a shebang, call the shell binary, or be owner-readable only; those are common misconceptions about profile files.

The shebang (#! line) tells the kernel which interpreter and optional arguments should run the script when it is executed directly. It is also treated as a comment by the interpreter itself, so the interpreter ignores that line during execution. It does not block execution, install software, or define character encoding.

The seq command with three numeric arguments prints a sequence from the first value to the third, incrementing by the second value. seq 1 5 20 therefore produces 1, 6, 11, and 16. When shown concatenated without separators, that output appears as 161116.

The Bash builtin set, when used without arguments, lists all shell variables and functions in the current session. env and env -a show environment variables passed to child processes, not Bash functions. export and echo $ENV do not provide a complete listing of defined variables and functions.

chage (change age) manages password aging policy for a user account, such as maximum password lifetime, minimum days between changes, and warning periods before expiry. It does not modify file ownership, group membership, default permissions, or the user's available commands.

ulimit is a shell builtin that sets per-process resource limits, including the maximum size of core dump files created when a program crashes. quota and edquota manage disk usage limits, while core and ktrace are unrelated to core-file size restrictions.

With shadow passwords, hashed credentials are stored in /etc/shadow, which is readable only by root, instead of in the world-readable /etc/passwd. This prevents regular users from obtaining password hashes for offline cracking. Shadowing does not mandate 45-day expiry, use host keys, require public-key pairs, or store passwords in plain text.

TCP wrappers (libwrap) read /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny when each new connection attempt is made, so rule changes take effect immediately for new connections. There is no separate tcpd daemon to restart, and existing established sessions are not required to close first.

The private key must be kept secret because possession of it proves identity for key-based SSH login. Users can create multiple key pairs for one account, and the key pair should be generated by the account owner so the private key is never exposed during creation. The public key—not the private key—is copied to the remote server's authorized_keys file.

find / -user root -perm -4000 searches the filesystem for files owned by root with the SetUID bit set (octal 4000). The other options use invalid or nonexistent find syntax such as -mode, -setuid, -permbits, or --filter.

ssh-add loads private keys into a running ssh-agent so they can be used for authentication without repeatedly entering passphrases. Keys are added to the agent process that is already running in the user's session, not by copying them to the remote server.

xinetd reads individual service definitions from /etc/xinetd.d/, where each file configures one super-server-managed service. The main xinetd configuration file is /etc/xinetd.conf, but per-service settings live in the xinetd.d directory.

ssh clients locate a running ssh-agent through environment variables such as SSH_AUTH_SOCK (Unix domain socket path) and SSH_AGENT_PID. They do not connect to a fixed port 2222, use a hard-coded .ssh-agent/ipc path, spawn a new agent per invocation, or rely on shell aliases.

The ssh -i option specifies the identity file (private key) to use for authentication instead of the default ~/.ssh/id_rsa or other standard key paths. Only the option name -i is required here, without a path or key filename.

IPv6 removed the concept of broadcast addresses; one-to-many delivery uses multicast instead. IPv4 addresses are not natively usable in IPv6 without transition mechanisms like IPv4-mapped addresses, and TCP/UDP port numbers remain unchanged from IPv4.

nmcli device wifi connect WIFIoI tells NetworkManager to connect to the Wi-Fi network with SSID WIFIoI, creating a connection profile if one does not already exist. It does not create a hotspot, add an unconfigured virtual interface, or require a pre-existing profile named WIFIoI.

dig -t ns www.example.org queries DNS nameserver (NS) records for the domain, which typically lists authoritative name servers and their hostnames. dig -t mx returns mail exchangers, dig -t a returns address records, and dig -t soa returns the start-of-authority record.

ip link set dev eth0 up brings the eth0 network interface to the administratively up state so it can send and receive traffic. Without up, the interface remains down even if it is configured with an IP address.

NetworkManager reports overall connectivity as states such as none, portal, limited, and full. portal means a captive portal must be satisfied before full Internet access, and full means the system has working Internet connectivity. up, login-required, and firewalled are not standard NetworkManager connectivity states.

A /28 subnet has 16 addresses; for 203.0.113.64/28 the network address is .64 and the broadcast is .79, so usable host addresses are .65 through .78. Therefore .65 and .78 are valid hosts, while .64 is the network address and .80/.81 fall in the next subnet.

/etc/resolv.conf commonly uses the nameserver directive to list DNS resolver addresses and the search directive to define domain suffixes for unqualified hostnames. substitute, lookup, and method are not standard resolv.conf keywords.

By default, ping sends ICMP Echo Request packets to the target host and waits for ICMP Echo Reply packets in return. It does not use ARP, TCP SYN probes, UDP port-0 probes, or network-wide broadcasts for its standard operation.

ifconfig eth0 and ip -s link show eth0 both display per-interface statistics including bytes transmitted (TX) and received (RX). route shows routing information, ip stats show is not a valid ip subcommand, and netstat -s -i does not accept an interface name in that form.

The routing table includes a more specific route for the 192.168.2.0/24 network via gateway 192.168.1.1 on eth0, so a packet to 192.168.2.150 matches that route rather than the default route on wlan0. The packet is therefore forwarded to 192.168.1.1 on eth0 instead of being sent directly on either interface.
